message.content:
Adversaries may exploit the Windows Command Shell (cmd) to execute commands and control systems. The command shell is a versatile tool on Windows systems, allowing for various levels of system control, including remote invocation via services like SSH. Batch files (.bat or .cmd) can automate repetitive tasks or execute commands across multiple systems. Common adversary tactics include using cmd to run single commands, interactively executing commands via command and control (C2) channels, and leveraging batch scripts for automation. Notable malware and threat groups, such as APT1, APT29, and FIN7, have utilized cmd for tasks ranging from payload execution to system reconnaissance and persistence.

The potential impact includes unauthorized system control, data exfiltration, and disruption of services. Mitigation strategies involve restricting command-line access, monitoring and logging command-line activity, employing application whitelisting, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect and block suspicious command-line usage. Regularly updating and patching systems, along with user training on phishing and social engineering, can also reduce the risk of exploitation.
